issues.description: The iterative lowering algorithm is fundamentally broken — it has no mechanism to wire child results back into parent nodes. The `results` vector is a flat list with no parent-child association. For a binary expression `a + b`, the method pushes a recursive result for the root AND pushes children onto the stack. Children are then processed independently, yielding orphaned results. `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` returns the last-processed leaf node's result, not the root expression. This will produce incorrect ASTs (returning a random child expression instead of the full tree) if the method is ever called.
issues.suggested_fix: A correct iterative tree builder requires a two-phase approach: (1) a work stack with frame types distinguishing 'visit' from 'combine' operations, and (2) an operand/results stack where combine frames pop their children's results and construct the parent node. See the Tarjan SCC pattern in `crates/reify-compiler/src/scc.rs:131-187` for the codebase's established iterative stack-frame pattern. Alternatively, use a simpler depth-limited recursion approach like `MAX_ALIAS_INSTANTIATION_DEPTH` in `crates/reify-compiler/src/lib.rs:1305` which already exists in the codebase for similar overflow protection.

issues.description: Binary expression handling double-processes children: it pushes children onto the iterative stack AND calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` which recursively processes the same children. This means (1) children are lowered twice (wasted work), (2) the recursive call defeats the stated purpose of avoiding stack overflow, and (3) the orphaned child results pushed to `results` corrupt the output. The comment acknowledges the problem ('Can't construct BinaryExpr yet — children not lowered / Just lower it recursively as a fallback') but the code proceeds with both paths anyway.
issues.suggested_fix: Either implement proper iterative bottom-up construction with combine frames, or remove the child-pushing code and only use the recursive fallback (making the iterative wrapper honest about what it does).

issues.description: Parenthesized expression handling pushes children to the stack and `continue`s without producing a result for the parent frame. The child is then processed as a standalone expression with no connection to the parenthesized context. Additionally, the filter `child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` is redundant — `is_named()` already excludes punctuation tokens like parentheses in tree-sitter, so this guard never filters anything.
issues.suggested_fix: For parenthesized expressions, the correct approach is the same as in `lower_expr`: find the single named child and recurse/iterate into it. If using depth-limited recursion instead, this case is handled naturally.

issues.description: The method `lower_expr_iterative` is never called anywhere in the codebase. It is dead code. Nothing invokes it, so the stack overflow problem it aims to solve remains unsolved. If it's intended for future use, it should at minimum have a test or a caller; otherwise it will bit-rot.
issues.suggested_fix: Either wire this into the call sites that need stack overflow protection (e.g., replace `lower_expr` calls for top-level expressions) or remove it until a correct implementation is ready.

issues.description: The method falls back to recursive calls (`lower_binary_expr`, `lower_unary_expr`, `lower_function_call`, and the catch-all `lower_expr`) for all compound expression types, completely defeating the iterative purpose. Additionally, it only covers 8 of the 22 node kinds handled by `lower_expr` — missing `range_expression`, `conditional_expression`, `match_expression`, `lambda_expression`, `quantifier_expression`, `quantity_literal`, `list_literal`, `set_literal`, `map_literal`, `ad_hoc_selector`, `index_access`, `member_access`, `qualified_access`, and `instance_qualified_access`. Any of these nested deeply would still overflow the stack.
issues.suggested_fix: The codebase already has two established patterns for stack overflow prevention: (1) explicit stack frames (scc.rs Tarjan), (2) depth-limited recursion with a constant (MAX_ALIAS_INSTANTIATION_DEPTH, MAX_MEMBER_NESTING_DEPTH). A depth-limited wrapper around `lower_expr` would be simpler, cover all 22 node kinds uniformly, and be consistent with existing codebase patterns.

opus_strategic.summary: The `lower_expr_iterative` method is algorithmically broken at a fundamental level. It attempts to convert recursive tree lowering to an iterative stack-based approach, but lacks the critical mechanism to associate child results with parent nodes — the results vector is flat with no frame structure. The method actually makes things worse: binary expressions are double-processed (once recursively, once via the stack), `results.last()` returns a leaf node instead of the root, and most compound types fall back to full recursion anyway, defeating the stated goal. The codebase already has two proven patterns for this problem (explicit stack frames in scc.rs, depth-limited recursion in the compiler), neither of which is followed here. The method is also dead code — nothing calls it. Recommend either implementing depth-limited recursion (simpler, covers all 22 node kinds uniformly) or a proper two-stack iterative builder following the Tarjan SCC pattern.

issues.description: The new `lower_expr_iterative` method has zero test coverage. The existing test suite has 58 tests for expression parsing but none were added for this new code path. Because the method is also never called from any production code path, it will never be exercised by integration tests either.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated `#[cfg(test)]` block with at minimum: (1) a basic leaf test (number literal returns correct Expr), (2) a binary expression test, (3) a parenthesized expression test, and (4) a deeply-nested expression test that would trigger stack overflow on the recursive path — since that is the stated motivation.

issues.description: The `binary_expression` arm calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` (which is fully recursive) and pushes that result into `results`, then also pushes the node's children onto the stack. Those children get processed later and their results appear *after* the binary expression result in `results`. Because the function returns `results.into_iter().last()`, it returns a child's result rather than the root binary expression. A simple test like `parse '1 + 2', expect BinaryExpr(Add, 1, 2)` would immediately fail, exposing this bug.
issues.suggested_fix: For the iterative approach to be correct, the binary expression result must be computed *after* its children are lowered (bottom-up). Either use a proper two-pass approach with a continuation stack, or — if falling back to recursive is intentional — ensure the result is marked as the root result and child pushes are removed. Add a test asserting that `lower_expr_iterative` on `1 + 2` returns `Expr::Binary(Add, Lit(1), Lit(2))`.

issues.description: `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` returns the last result pushed, not the root expression's result. For any non-leaf root (binary, parenthesized with inner child), descendant nodes are pushed after the root, processed later, and their results appear last in `results`. There is no test asserting that the return value of `lower_expr_iterative(root)` equals `lower_expr(root)` for any input, so this systematic inversion goes undetected.
issues.suggested_fix: Add an equivalence test that runs both `lower_expr` and `lower_expr_iterative` on the same node and asserts their outputs are identical. This would immediately flag the result-ordering bug. The result-selection logic should be changed to capture only the root's result (e.g., compute it before pushing children, or use a separate `root_result` variable).

issues.description: The function's doc comment states it avoids stack overflow on deeply nested expressions, but there is no test demonstrating this property — neither a regression test showing the recursive version overflows, nor a test showing the iterative version succeeds on, say, 10 000 levels of nesting. Without such a test, the correctness and necessity of this code cannot be verified.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test that constructs a deeply nested expression (e.g., `((((... x ...))))` with 5000+ levels or a left-recursive chain of binary adds) and asserts `lower_expr_iterative` returns `Some(_)`. This documents the motivation and guards against regressions.

issues.description: The `parenthesized_expression` arm uses `continue` to skip pushing a result for the parenthesized wrapper, relying on the child's result to bubble up last. This is untested. For a root node that is a `parenthesized_expression` containing only a number literal, the behavior depends entirely on the LIFO ordering — a test would verify (or falsify) that the inner expression is returned correctly.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test: `lower_expr_iterative` on a `parenthesized_expression` node wrapping `42` should return `Expr::Literal(42)`, same as `lower_expr`.

issues.description: `lower_expr_iterative` is never called from any production code path (confirmed by codebase search). Without an integration test or call site, even a passing unit test suite cannot verify the function participates correctly in the parse pipeline.
issues.suggested_fix: Either wire `lower_expr_iterative` into the parse pipeline (e.g., as the implementation of `lower_expr` with a depth guard) and add an integration test, or add a `// TODO: integrate` comment and a tracking issue. As written, the function is untestable through normal parse flows.

issues.description: The `_` catch-all arm calls `self.lower_expr(node)` recursively, which is the exact behavior the function is meant to replace. There is no test verifying which node kinds fall through to this arm, nor a test confirming that the recursive fallback produces correct results within the iterative framing (where the fallback result also ends up in `results` and competes with other entries).
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test with a node kind that hits the `_` arm (e.g., `conditional_expression` or `lambda_expression`) and assert the returned value matches `lower_expr` on the same node.

sonnet_test_analyst.summary: The diff introduces `lower_expr_iterative` with zero test coverage: no unit tests, no integration tests, and no call sites that would exercise it indirectly. More critically, two blocking logic bugs are present that any test would immediately catch. First, the `binary_expression` arm calls the fully-recursive `lower_binary_expr` and then pushes children onto the stack; those children are processed after the root, so their results appear last in `results`, and `results.into_iter().last()` returns a child expression rather than the root — making every non-leaf invocation return the wrong value. Second, this result-selection strategy (`last()`) is fundamentally incompatible with a LIFO stack where children are pushed after the parent is processed: the root result is always overwritten by descendant results for any compound expression. Three additional suggestions cover: the missing motivating deep-nesting stress test (the stated reason for the function's existence), the untested `parenthesized_expression` `continue` path, and the `_` fallback that silently re-enters the recursive implementation. The function should not be merged without at minimum a leaf test, a binary expression test, and an equivalence test against `lower_expr`.

issues.description: The function returns `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` — the *last* item appended to results, which is the last leaf processed, not the root expression. For a `binary_expression` root: (1) root is popped first, its result from `lower_binary_expr` is pushed to `results[0]`; (2) the children pushed onto the stack are then each processed and their results appended after. `last()` therefore returns the last child's result (typically the left operand), not the root binary expression. For any non-leaf root, the return value is always wrong.
issues.suggested_fix: A correct iterative post-order approach requires a two-phase stack (push a 'visit' marker, then a 'build' marker) or a separate 'pending parents' structure so that child results can be consumed to construct the parent. Alternatively, remove the function entirely and increase the system stack size via `stacker` or a thread with a larger stack.

issues.description: In the `binary_expression` arm, children are pushed onto the stack *and* `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` is called recursively in the same arm. `lower_binary_expr` already recursively lowers its children via `lower_expr`. The stack-pushed children are then separately processed by the while loop, appending their individual results to `results` *after* the already-correct root result. This guarantees the `last()` return picks up a leaf child result rather than the compound expression. The stack pushes are not just useless — they actively break correctness.
issues.suggested_fix: Either call `lower_binary_expr` recursively and do not push children (accepting that stack overflow is not solved for this node kind), or implement a true two-phase iterative descent where children are pushed without immediate recursive processing and the parent node is reconstructed from child results in a second pass.

issues.description: The function's docstring promises to avoid stack overflow by using an explicit stack instead of recursion, but every non-leaf branch still makes recursive calls: `binary_expression` calls `lower_binary_expr` → `lower_expr` recursively; `unary_expression` calls `lower_unary_expr` → `lower_expr`; `function_call` calls `lower_function_call` → `lower_expr`; the `_` fallback calls `lower_expr` directly. A maximally nested binary expression tree will still exhaust the native call stack in exactly the same way as the original `lower_expr`. The iterative wrapper provides zero protection.
issues.suggested_fix: To genuinely prevent stack overflow, none of the node handlers invoked inside the loop may recursively call any `lower_*` function. All tree traversal must go through the explicit stack. Use a two-phase approach: push `(node, Phase::Descend)` initially; when descending, push the parent as `(node, Phase::Build)` then push all children as `Descend`; when building, pop N child results from a value stack to construct the parent expression.

issues.description: `parenthesized_expression` uses `continue` to skip the `results.push(expr)` call. For the root node this accidentally works (the inner child becomes the last result), but in any hypothetical correct post-order build the parenthesized node would leave a hole in the results sequence — no result is produced for it, so a parent trying to consume its result would consume the wrong sibling result instead.
issues.suggested_fix: In a corrected two-phase implementation, the parenthesized expression's 'build' phase should simply pop one child result from the value stack and re-push it (or substitute its own span), not skip result production entirely.

issues.description: `child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` is redundant. In tree-sitter, `(` and `)` are anonymous tokens; `child.is_named()` already excludes them, so the explicit kind checks are dead code that adds confusion.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the `&& child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` conditions; rely solely on `child.is_named()`.

issues.description: `lower_expr_iterative` is not called from anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by search). It is unreachable dead code from the moment it is merged, meaning the correctness bugs above will never be caught by tests until a call site is added.
issues.suggested_fix: Either add a `#[allow(dead_code)]` annotation with a TODO comment explaining it is under construction, or add a call site before merging so the function is exercised by the test suite.

sonnet_robustness.summary: The new `lower_expr_iterative` function has three blocking correctness/safety defects. First, its return value is structurally wrong: it returns `results.last()`, but for any compound root node the root's result is pushed first and child results are appended after, so `last()` returns a leaf child rather than the root expression. Second, the `binary_expression` arm both pushes children onto the stack and immediately calls `lower_binary_expr` recursively — the recursive call already processes the children, so the stack-pushed children are then double-processed, appending spurious leaf results that worsen the wrong-return bug. Third, the stated goal of preventing stack overflow is not met: every non-leaf node kind still invokes a fully recursive `lower_*` helper, so deeply nested trees will overflow the native call stack just as before. Two additional suggestions: the `child.kind() != "("` checks in the `parenthesized_expression` arm are dead code (anonymous nodes are already excluded by `is_named()`), and the function itself is unreachable dead code with no call site, meaning none of these bugs will surface in the test suite until a call site is added.
